The paper tools aren't returning relevant kinetics literature. The mechanistic principle is well-established regardless: in the Knowles/Cohen master-equation framework of Abeta42 aggregation, small-molecule inhibitors often act selectively — many bind fibril surfaces to suppress **secondary nucleation** (the autocatalytic monomer-dependent generation of new nuclei on existing fibril surfaces), while elongation proceeds. The breakthrough idea instead posits inhibitor blocking primary/nucleation lag while elongation is unaffected. Both are supported structurally by the same "separate the microscopic processes and inhibit one selectively" principle.

I'll implement the Finke-Watzky two-step structure with monomer conservation, inhibitor acting on the nucleation channel only. I noted local research tools returned no useful kinetics papers, so I'm proceeding from the established master-equation principle.
